attributes.stem: A 45-year-old man attends a follow-up clinic for management of his schizophrenia. He has received therapy with quetiapine, risperidone and paliperidone over the last two years, with limited success. He reports ongoing paranoid delusions around spy agencies conducting covert operations in his backyard, as well as troubling auditory hallucinations that prevent him from sleeping at night. In his most recent medical review, his psychiatrist suggested that clozapine treatment may be appropriate. He is a smoker and drinks alcohol daily. In counselling for initiation of clozapine therapy, a number of issues are discussed, including the monitoring requirements whilst on this medication.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is a monitoring requirement for patients on clozapine?
options: Baseline EEG to assess seizure risk
Weekly white cell count monitoring for the first 18 weeks
Monthly serum troponin T levels for the first 52 weeks
Finger prick blood glucose at each clinical review
attributes.explanation:
The correct answer to this question is weekly white cell count monitoring for the first 18 weeks.

This patient presents with ongoing symptoms of schizophrenia that have thus far, been refractory to medical treatment. As such, he is a suitable candidate for clozapine therapy. Whilst clozapine is the most effective antipsychotic agent for management of schizophrenia, it is associated with a range of side effects. As such, there are a number of monitoring requirements that must be adhered to, in order to ensure patient safety.

Agranulocytosis is one of the most serious side effects that can occur with therapy, as it puts patients at risk of life-threatening infections. As such, monitoring of the white cell count is important, particularly when initiating treatment. In Australia, the current recommendation is weekly white cell count monitoring for the first 18 weeks of therapy, making this the correct answer. After this period, monitoring can be less frequent (every 28 days) as the risk is highest in the initial phase of the prescription.

Whilst clozapine is associated with an increased risk of seizures, a baseline EEG is not a standard monitoring requirement, making this answer incorrect.

Myocarditis and cardiac failure are both associated with clozapine therapy. Due to this, all patients must undergo an echocardiogram at baseline to ensure any pre-existing cardiac dysfunction is diagnosed prior to commencing clozapine. There is no need to subject the patient to a trans-oesophageal echocardiogram to assess cardiac function, as a simple transthoracic echocardiogram is more than adequate. Therefore, trans-oesophageal echocardiogram at baseline is not the best answer.

As mentioned above, myocarditis can occur with clozapine therapy, so in addition to baseline echocardiography, patients must also undergo regular serum troponin testing to assess for any elevation. The current recommendation is troponin testing at day 7, 14, 21 and 28, again at week 12, and then yearly thereafter. Monthly serum troponin T levels for the first 52 weeks describes a far more intensive monitoring strategy than currently suggested, making this answer option incorrect. It is important to note that CRP levels and ECGs are also performed to screen for the development of myocarditis and cardiomyopathy.

Like many antipsychotics from the atypical (2nd generation) class, clozapine can cause a number of metabolic side effects, including hypertension, insulin resistance, weight gain and dyslipidaemia. It is important to monitor for the development of these side effects, as they can be particularly distressing for patients. Despite this however, there is currently no formal requirement to measure finger prick blood glucose at every clinical review. The current recommendation is around once every 3 months.
learningPoints: Understanding of the monitoring requirements for patients commencing clozapine therapy
references: SA Health. Clozapine management clinical guideline [internet]. [Adelaide]: SA Health [updated 2017 Sep; cited 2021 Jul 31]. (Policy no.: CG260). Available from:https://www.sahealth.sa.gov.au/wps/wcm/connect/059961804298b3078c77be80c298878e/Clozapine+Management+Clinical+Guideline_13092017.pdf?MOD=AJPERES&CACHEID=ROOTWORKSPACE-059961804298b3078c77be80c298878e-nzp2rc3 [updated 2022 guideline - https://www.sahealth.sa.gov.au/wps/wcm/connect/059961804298b3078c77be80c298878e/Clozapine+Management+Clinical+Guideline+_2.0_final.pdf?MOD=AJPERES&CACHEID=ROOTWORKSPACE-059961804298b3078c77be80c298878e-oe6.0nY]
